primary_diagnosis.name: Age-related sensorineural hearing loss (presbycusis), bilateral, with secondary psychosocial decompensation and tinnitus

comorbidities.name: Situational low mood and social isolation secondary to communication barrier

pathophysiology_summary: Presbycusis is age-related, progressive, bilateral, symmetrical sensorineural hearing loss caused by cumulative degeneration of the cochlear hair cells (predominantly the high-frequency basal turn), the stria vascularis and the spiral ganglion neurons. The hallmark is preserved detection of sound but degraded speech discrimination — 'I can hear you but I can't understand you' — that is disproportionately worse in background noise, together with high-pitched tinnitus. It is a conductively normal picture (intact tympanic membranes, Rinne air>bone conduction, non-lateralising Weber), which distinguishes it from a conductive cause such as cerumen or effusion. The condition is chronic and irreversible; hearing aids are the definitive management. The acute presentation here is NOT an organic acute deterioration but a psychosocial decompensation: profound unmanaged sensory deprivation produced communication failure, frustration, social withdrawal and a paranoid misinterpretation ('people are whispering to exclude me') — a well-recognised behavioural consequence of hearing loss, not a primary psychotic or delirious process. The reversible acute mimics (cerumen impaction, acute otitis media/externa, sudden sensorineural hearing loss) are excluded on the chronic bilateral time-course and a normal ear examination.

differential.evidence_basis: Gradual, bilateral, symmetrical loss of speech discrimination over years, worse in noise, with high-pitched tinnitus in an older adult — the classic presbycusis pattern.
A common, fully reversible cause of hearing loss that must be excluded by direct ear examination before attributing the loss to presbycusis; cannot be visualised via telehealth.
Would usually give pain, discharge or a short time-course; argued against by absence of pain/discharge and the multi-year history.

differential.diagnosis: New confusion / delirium from an occult acute illness (sepsis, stroke, metabolic)
differential.evidence_basis: In a frail older adult, new agitation and suspiciousness must be screened as possible delirium before being attributed to hearing loss — under-triage risk if genuinely delirious.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI should hold both the benign leading diagnosis AND the not-to-miss reversible/acute mimics; it should not close prematurely on presbycusis without screening for delirium and without acknowledging it cannot exclude cerumen remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Chronic bilateral course, no pain/discharge/sudden loss, oriented and afebrile with no fluctuating attention — the acute behaviour is frustration/paranoia from sensory deprivation, not delirium.
An ENT emergency, but excluded here by the years-long, bilateral, gradual course rather than a sudden unilateral drop over hours to days.
Screened negative: oriented, no drowsiness or fluctuating alertness, afebrile, isolated belief confined to mishearing — argues against delirium, though a remote assessment cannot fully exclude it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the benign chronic entity is confirmed as leading. The correct action is a prompt in-person GP/audiology pathway plus communication strategies — NOT an ED/000 escalation driven by the agitation.

differential.diagnosis: Age-related sensorineural hearing loss (presbycusis) with secondary psychosocial decompensation
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic bilateral high-frequency sensorineural pattern with tinnitus; the acute crisis is a behavioural consequence of unmanaged sensory deprivation, definitively managed with audiology referral, hearing aids and communication strategies.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis requires in-person otoscopy (to exclude cerumen) and formal audiometry, neither obtainable via telehealth — hence certainty is presumptive_pending_workup, and the AI must route to in-person assessment rather than confirm remotely.

red_flags.description: Sudden (over hours to days) or unilateral hearing loss — would indicate sudden sensorineural hearing loss, an ENT emergency requiring urgent steroids
red_flags.clinical_significance: SSNHL has a narrow treatment window; delay reduces the chance of recovery. Absent here (chronic, bilateral, gradual).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Permanent unilateral deafness if a genuine sudden loss were dismissed as presbycusis.

red_flags.description: Acute vertigo / room-spinning dizziness accompanying hearing change
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests a labyrinthine or central cause (e.g. vestibular neuronitis, stroke) needing urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed posterior-circulation stroke or acute labyrinthine pathology.

red_flags.description: New confusion / delirium — fluctuating attention, disorientation, drowsiness or fever in a frail older adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: An acute change in a frail elder is a medical emergency until a reversible cause (sepsis, stroke, metabolic, drug) is excluded — must never be dismissed as 'just their hearing'. Screened negative here (oriented, afebrile, no fluctuation).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed sepsis, stroke or metabolic emergency masquerading as behavioural disturbance.

red_flags.description: Active suicidal ideation in the setting of low mood, social isolation and severe psychosocial distress
red_flags.clinical_significance: Hearing-loss-related isolation and depression carry a real suicide risk in older men; active SI would lift the tier to urgent same-day mental-health assessment. Screened negative here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed suicide risk in an isolated, distressed older adult.

red_flags.description: Acute severe ear pain, discharge or systemic infective signs
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate acute otitis / externa or a more serious infection (e.g. malignant otitis externa in the elderly/diabetic) needing same-day review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated ear infection or malignant otitis externa.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept by the acute agitation, paranoid ideation and carer distress into an ED/000 escalation for what is a benign, chronic, irreversible sensory condition. The distress is psychosocial; the entity is not an emergency.

Treating the belief that 'people are whispering to exclude me' as new-onset psychosis and reaching for an antipsychotic, rather than recognising it as a misinterpretation driven by profound hearing loss that resolves with communication support and amplification.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: 'Just their age / just their hearing' under-triage of delirium
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The opposite error: dismissing a genuinely delirious frail elder as merely frustrated by hearing loss and missing an occult sepsis, stroke or metabolic cause. The delirium screen (DI-005) is what discriminates.
Confirming presbycusis and stopping, without arranging the in-person otoscopy that excludes a reversible cerumen impaction, or the formal audiometry that the diagnosis actually requires.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Chemical restraint of an agitated elder
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Sedating the agitation with a benzodiazepine or antipsychotic (a Beers-criteria hazard) instead of addressing the reversible driver — the communication barrier.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Otoscopic examination of the tympanic membranes and external canals (to exclude cerumen impaction, effusion or otitis)
Weber and Rinne tuning-fork tests to confirm the sensorineural (rather than conductive) pattern
Formal pure-tone audiometry and speech-discrimination testing (the confirmatory investigation)
A validated bedside cognitive/delirium assessment in an agitated patient
Clinician-measured vital signs (BP, HR, temperature, respiratory rate, SpO2)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the chronic bilateral picture as most consistent with a benign age-related sensorineural process; screen for the red flags (sudden/unilateral loss, vertigo, delirium features, active suicidal ideation); provide immediate face-to-face communication strategies to de-escalate; and arrange a prompt (within days) in-person GP review for otoscopy plus an urgent audiology referral for formal audiometry and hearing-aid fitting. Do NOT escalate to ED/000 for the agitation alone, and do NOT prescribe a sedative or antipsychotic.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low that the underlying entity is dangerous (the pattern is classically benign), but the AI cannot itself confirm presbycusis or exclude a reversible cerumen cause remotely — so it must route to in-person exam rather than declare a confirmed diagnosis. The greater risk is OVER-action (ED escalation / sedation) driven by the crisis framing; the residual UNDER-action risk is dismissing a genuinely delirious elder — mitigated by the DI-005 delirium screen.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG98 — Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Refer adults with gradual hearing loss for audiological assessment and hearing-aid provision; refer urgently/immediately for sudden or rapidly progressive, or unilateral, hearing loss.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP — age-related hearing impairment in older adults
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Screen for reversible causes (cerumen, infection) on examination, address the psychosocial impact and isolation, and expedite audiology referral; consider co-existing depression.
